old_claim.note: Blast Furnace provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Plate armor's spread depended on faster, cheaper plate forming; Britannica specifically links medieval plate armor to water-powered trip hammers.

source_shape.source_locator: History section: Britannica says plate armour became widespread in medieval Europe when water-powered trip hammers made forming plates faster and cheaper.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Plate armor depended directly on forming large articulated plates, and Britannica singles out water-powered trip hammers for that process.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Blast furnaces supplied broader iron-production capacity, but the source-pinned plate-armor mechanism is plate forming by powered hammers, which better matches the target node.

ontology_before: Plate Armor depended directly on blast_furnace, implying blast-furnace iron production was the direct technological gate for full articulated plate harness.
ontology_after: Plate Armor depends on water-powered trip hammers for plate forming, advanced medieval metallurgy for iron/steel treatment, and mail smithing for flexible joint protection; blast furnaces remain upstream or scaling context.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: plate_armor now depends on water_powered_trip_hammers_forges and no longer directly depends on blast_furnace.
would_reject_if: The node is later narrowed to blast-furnace-origin steel supply rather than full plate-harness fabrication.
A stronger source directly shows blast_furnace was required for the first full articulated plate harnesses.
A dedicated armor_plate_rolling or armorer_plate_forming node is added and should replace water_powered_trip_hammers_forges.
short_reason: Powered plate-forming is the source-backed direct edge; blast furnace was too broad and upstream.
